disclaimer: This information is for general education only. It is not a diagnosis, prescription, or medical advice. Medicines and doses must be decided by a qualified prescriber who knows your full history. In an emergency, call your local emergency number.

conditions.summary: Long-term high blood sugar (hyperglycemia) can affect nerves, blood vessels, eyes, and kidneys. Good control combines healthy eating, activity, regular monitoring, and medications when prescribed.
selfCare: Follow meal patterns and portions your care team recommends (often: more vegetables, fewer sugary drinks, consistent carb timing if advised).
Move most days: walking and light activity as cleared by your clinician.
Check blood sugar if you were given a glucometer, and keep a log if asked.
Foot care: inspect feet daily; seek care for cuts that do not heal.
whenToSeeDoctor: First diagnosis or if numbers stay high despite lifestyle changes — medication may be needed.
Symptoms of very high or very low blood sugar, vomiting, or confusion — urgent care.
Pregnancy or planning pregnancy — care must be specialist-led.
conditions.medicationNote: Many people with type 2 diabetes use one or more of: metformin, other oral medicines, non-insulin injectables, or insulin. Doses, timing, and whether a drug is safe for you depend on kidney function, other conditions, and interactions — only your prescriber should set or change them.

medicationClasses.name: Metformin (common first-line for many adults with type 2)
medicationClasses.typicalUse: Often used to improve insulin sensitivity. Starting dose, maximum dose, and whether it is safe for you are decided with blood tests and your doctor — do not copy someone else's dose.

medicationClasses.name: Insulin and other options
medicationClasses.typicalUse: Some people need insulin or other classes of medicines. Dosing is highly individual and must be titrated with professional guidance.

conditions.summary: Sustained high blood pressure increases risk of stroke and heart disease. Management includes lifestyle changes and, when needed, long-term medication.
selfCare: Reduce salt, limit alcohol, maintain a healthy weight, and stay active as advised.
Home BP monitoring if your clinician recommended it, at the same time each day.
Take prescribed medicines on schedule — do not stop abruptly without medical advice.
whenToSeeDoctor: Very high readings with chest pain, bad headache, or neurological symptoms — emergency.
To choose or adjust medicines — class and dose need regular review.
conditions.medicationNote: There are several drug classes (e.g. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, diuretics, calcium-channel blockers). The right one for you depends on age, kidney function, and other health issues. Doses are titrated by your prescriber.

medicationClasses.name: Example classes (not a personal plan)
medicationClasses.typicalUse: Your doctor picks one or more classes and a starting dose, then follows up with measurements and side-effect checks.

conditions.summary: Fever is often the body's response to infection. Rest, fluids, and simple measures help; some medicines reduce discomfort when appropriate and safe for you.
selfCare: Light clothing; avoid ice baths. Watch for dehydration (dry mouth, little urine, dizziness).
whenToSeeDoctor: Infants with fever, or fever lasting more than a few days, or severe symptoms — seek care.
If you have liver, kidney, bleeding risk, or are on other drugs — check before any medicine.
conditions.medicationNote: Fever reducers and pain relievers (e.g. paracetamol/acetaminophen class) are used in many guidelines. Dose by weight/age in children and with dose limits in adults — use the leaflet or ask a pharmacist. Avoid doubling up products that already contain the same drug.

medicationClasses.name: Paracetamol / acetaminophen (illustrative, not a prescription)
medicationClasses.typicalUse: Common adult maximum daily amount is set on the product; liver disease and alcohol use change safety — confirm with a clinician or label.

conditions.summary: Stomach acid irritating the oesophagus can cause burning. Lifestyle helps; persistent symptoms may need prescribed therapy.
selfCare: Smaller meals; avoid late heavy dinners; limit triggers you notice (e.g. spicy, fatty foods).
Elevate head of bed if night symptoms.
whenToSeeDoctor: Difficulty swallowing, weight loss, black stools, or severe pain — urgent assessment.
Long-standing symptoms — may need endoscopy or prescription treatment.
conditions.medicationNote: Antacids, H2 blockers, and proton-pump inhibitors are used in different situations. Long-term use of some classes has risks; duration and dose should be guided by a prescriber.

medicationClasses.name: PPIs and related (example category)
medicationClasses.typicalUse: Chronic use and step-down are decided with your doctor to balance benefit and side effects.
